prompt: I have just joined this monorepo. Map its workspaces, startup entry points, authoritative test commands, and the checkout request path. Keep the work read-only.
Inspect this unfamiliar service and tell me where a CSV export feature belongs, which components it would cross, and which tests cover that path. Do not change files.
Bootstrap shared project context for this existing service: agent guidance, conventions, architecture views, and an ADR structure. Preserve and follow any documentation already present.
Use the EE control-plane workflow to establish the architecture and engineering conventions for a new event-booking platform before feature implementation starts.
We want notifications somehow, but the request is still vague. Use EE clarify to turn it into a scoped work item with testable acceptance criteria and open questions.
Clarify this rough bug request before anyone plans it: customers sometimes see stale totals after changing quantity. Define expected behavior, boundaries, and acceptance criteria.
Use EE breakdown to split the approved SSO migration into small independently testable work items with dependencies and acceptance criteria.
This checkout epic is already agreed but too large. Prepare Ralph-sized iterations without implementing or running Ralph yet.
Write an ADR recording why we chose PostgreSQL over DynamoDB, including the alternatives, rationale, consequences, and follow-up work. Follow this repository's ADR convention.
Supersede our accepted synchronous-messaging ADR with the new queue-based decision and preserve the historical relationship between the records.
Apply the Equal Experts LLM toolkit to this repository as a versioned rule library, then select the TypeScript and testing rules relevant to the project.
For one-off guidance, identify the EE rule files that should constrain upcoming REST API work. Do not install anything or change the repository.
Use Ralph with the Codex backend to make a plan for specs/payments.md. Summarize the highest-priority item, but do not start a build.
Run one Ralph build iteration from the existing IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md, inspect the result and tests, and do not push.
Investigate the intermittent timeout in the invoice worker. Reproduce it, isolate the cause, and recommend a regression test, but do not implement a fix.
This test passes locally and fails only in CI. Preserve the original error and compare the environments until you can confirm the cause.
Audit outdated npm dependencies across the workspaces and propose small compatible update batches. Do not update anything yet.
Upgrade this project from Django 5.1 to 5.2, assess the release-note impact, update the lockfile, and run the relevant checks.
Run every repository-defined formatter check, linter, type check, test suite, and build, then report PASS, FAIL, or INCONCLUSIVE. Do not fix failures.
Are these local changes ready to merge? Execute the required CI-equivalent checks and report the exact failing commands, if any.
Review the staged, unstaged, and untracked changes for concrete correctness, security, and regression defects. Report findings without modifying files.
Compare this topic branch with its merge base and review the patch for actionable defects and missing tests; do not run the full project gate.
Create two atomic local commits from my current work: one for the parser fix and one for its tests. Follow the repository's message convention and stop after committing.
Commit only the documentation changes that are already complete. Leave the unrelated source edits untouched and do not push.
Push the current topic branch to its configured upstream. Do not create a commit or open a pull request.
Rebase this unpublished topic branch onto the repository's verified default branch, preserving the dirty worktree and reporting any ambiguity before mutation.
Assess whether version 2.0 is ready to tag and publish, including versions, release notes, compatibility, migrations, artifacts, operational risk, and rollback. Do not publish it.
Evaluate this deployment candidate for release blockers: included scope, configuration changes, migration order, observability, and rollback evidence. Reuse matching test evidence where possible.
Draft a friendly two-paragraph blog announcement for our summer office hours from the notes in this prompt.
Explain in plain language what a database index is and give me one small SQL example.
